summary: Jeff Atwood breaks his 14-year silence on politics to address what he sees as a crisis in American democracy following the 2016 election. He rejects specific policies including the US-Mexico border wall and the Muslim travel ban, arguing they contradict America's founding values as a nation of immigrants. The post serves as both a personal reckoning—acknowledging his own passive relationship with democracy—and a call to action for civic engagement. Atwood outlines concrete steps he's taking, from calling representatives to donating to organizations like the ACLU and investigative journalism outlets. He distinguishes between nationalism, which he views as destructive, and the underlying American ideals of freedom, justice, and equality.
key_points: After 46 years of passively benefiting from democracy, Atwood recognizes the need to actively defend it through civic engagement
The post explicitly rejects both the border wall and Muslim ban policies while celebrating Mexican and Muslim immigrants as embodying American values
Atwood provides a detailed action plan: calling representatives, supporting journalism, donating to civil liberties organizations, and considering running for office
He draws a sharp distinction between harmful nationalism and the legitimate ideals (freedom, justice, equality) that America was founded on
The post frames this moment as potentially the most important time for activism since the civil rights movement
money_quotes: In my 14 years of blogging, I've never written a political blog post. I haven't needed to. Until now.

Imagine the bravery it takes to leave everything behind, your relatives, your home, your whole life as you know it, to take your entire family on a five thousand mile journey to another country on nothing more than the promise of a dream.
Muslim immigrants are more American than I will ever be, and I am incredibly proud to have them here, as fellow Americans.
We brought fact checkers to a culture war… and we lost.
I have benefited from democracy passively, without trying at all, for 46 years.
I pledge my allegiance to the American dream, and the American people—not to the nation, never to the nation.
I apologize that it's taken me 46 years to wake up and realize that some things, like the American dream, aren't guaranteed.
key_insight: Democracy requires active participation, not passive consumption—the American dream must be continually defended through civic engagement rather than taken for granted.
